title: Accessible State Review
prompt: Review an editor control that toggles a live preview and stores block attributes. Explain the state model, keyboard behavior, labels, announcements if needed, and how the frontend remains progressively enhanced.
hints: State that is easy for the model to describe can still be unusable if keyboard and screen reader behavior are neglected.

model_answer.summary: The editor control should have one clear source of truth, accessible controls, and a frontend that still works when JavaScript is unavailable.
model_answer.work: Store persistent settings in block attributes and use setAttributes for changes that must be saved. Use local state or useState only for temporary UI details, or a small @wordpress/data store when multiple editor components need the same state. Prefer @wordpress/components controls with explicit label text, predictable keyboard behavior, and focus that stays on the triggering control after a toggle. If the preview updates important text, add an aria-live announcement or screen reader status message. On the frontend, render the meaningful default HTML on the server, then add JavaScript as progressive enhancement so a server-rendered fallback remains usable.
checks: Verify saved attributes round-trip through the block editor.
Test keyboard-only operation for the toggle and preview controls.
Check screen reader labels, focus order, and any aria-live announcement.
Confirm @wordpress/components, @wordpress/element, and @wordpress/data are used only where they clarify the state model.
Disable frontend JavaScript and verify the fallback still communicates the content.
model_answer.why_this_is_good: It separates durable block data from temporary editor state and treats accessibility as part of the data flow, not as polish after the fact.
common_misses: Using local state for values that must be saved as attributes.
Relying on icon-only controls without labels.
Building a JavaScript-only frontend with no server-rendered fallback.
model_answer.revision_prompt: Revise by naming the state source, the accessibility behavior, the WordPress packages involved, and the no-JavaScript frontend fallback.
